title: Capturing non-Markovian dynamics in non-equilibrium stochastic systems using flow matching
authors: Bhargav Sriram Siddani, John B. Bell, Alejandro L. Garcia, Ishan Srivastava
abstract: Hydrodynamic models of stochastic particle systems represented by coarse-grained stochastic partial differential equations (SPDE), such as the regularized Dean-Kawasaki (DK) equation, do not accurately capture the short-time system dynamics that is dominated by non-Markovian effects, and low particle density regimes where the distributions are highly non-Gaussian. We develop a generative flow matching method that directly models the probability distribution of fluxes from particle simulations that explicitly incorporates non-Markovian and non-Gaussian effects. As a demonstration, we use this method to simulate the Kramers first passage time problem for a system of non-interacting Brownian particles. We show the model accurately captures the short-time behavior and provides better predictions of the statistical moments of the number density when compared against the solution of the Markovian baseline, regularized DK equation.
summary: This paper introduces a generative flow matching method to overcome the limitations of coarse-grained equations in modeling particle systems exhibiting strong non-Markovian and non-Gaussian behavior. By directly learning from particle flux distributions, the model accurately captures short-time dynamics for Brownian particles, outperforming standard Markovian approximations.
main_problem: The core challenge is accurately modeling non-Markovian dynamics and highly non-Gaussian effects in stochastic particle systems, which standard coarse-grained equations fail to capture at short times or low densities.
main_result: A generative flow matching method successfully captures the short-time behavior and higher-order statistical moments of particle density distributions more accurately than established Markovian models like the regularized Dean-Kawasaki equation.
method: The authors developed a generative flow matching (FM) technique that directly learns the probability distribution of fluxes by conditioning on the history of local states to incorporate memory effects.
model_or_system: The study uses non-interacting Brownian particles governed by an external potential, comparing results against particle simulations and coarse-grained Stochastic Partial Differential Equations (SPDEs).
key_observables: Spatial particle distribution, higher-order statistical moments of the number density in a target region, and Kramers first passage time statistics.
important_parameters: Short times ($t 	o 0$), low particle densities, and the memory length scale (captured by conditioning on previous states).
main_assumptions: The primary limitation noted is that model accuracy degrades at later simulation times; the regularized DK equation's failure to capture history is also a key assumption being tested.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares realizations of system state and time evolution of particle count statistics across three models (Brownian random walkers, regularized DK, and two FM models) at early times.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the limitations of existing SPDEs for non-Markovian dynamics. It then introduces and details the generative flow matching framework, applying it to simulate a known physical problem (Kramers passage time) and comparing its results against established benchmarks.
why_interesting: While focused on classical stochastic processes, the use of advanced machine learning techniques like Flow Matching to model complex non-equilibrium dynamics is highly relevant for simulating open quantum systems where memory effects are crucial.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The top row shows a subset of 100 independent one-dimensional realizations (along vertical direction) at time t = 5.1 × 10−3 simulated using Brownian random walkers, the regu- larized Dean–Kawasaki (DK) model, and the two flow matching (FM) models: Markovian and non-Markovian. The white patches denote negative values in the simulation. The bottom row com- pares the time evolution of the statistics of particle count in the target region around x = 0.7.

Figure 1 depicts independent realizations of an early-time state of the system. The regularized DK model results in high occurrence of nonphysical negative densities as compared to the ML models. While the DK model well-predicts mean particle densities in the target region, the non-Markovian ML model more accurately captures the spatial particle distribution and also the higher-order statistical moments (see bottom row), thus demonstrating significant non-Markovian effects at early times.
